title: Iwi affiliation (estimated counts): 2018 data sources and quality by iwi | Stats NZ
content:
## Iwi affiliation (estimated counts): 2018 data sources and quality by iwi
22 June 2021, 10:45am
This page provides information on data sources and quality for each iwi in the Iwi affiliation (estimated counts): 2018 release, by region.
For each region, we have provided:
- a graph to show the contribution of the different data sources for each iwi in the region
- a table of the 2013 Census count, the 2018 Census estimated count, and the percent change between these two counts for each iwi in the region
- information on any classification or coding changes.
Please consider the information about your iwi on this page when using the estimates to inform decisions, and in conjunction with the following:
- 2018 iwi affiliation estimated counts – Datainfo+ provides a more detailed description of the quality of the 2018 iwi estimates. It is recommended you read this document to help understand the information on this page.
- Strengths and limitations of 2018 iwi affiliation estimated counts provides guidance on how to use the estimates.
Changes between 2013 and 2018
For most iwi, the 2018 iwi estimates are between 20 and 40 percent larger than the 2013 Census counts. We do not recommend using the 2018 iwi estimates to measure population growth since 2013 due to a number of changes since the 2013 Census. The percent change provided for each iwi was used to identify iwi with larger or smaller growth, compared with expectations. Iwi are flagged in the quality statements below the tables where the 2018 estimate is less than 10 percent larger than the 2013 count or 60 percent larger than the 2013 count. Iwi added to the classification in the 2017 review do not have a population count in the 2013 Census and appear in the tables as 'Figure not available'.
The iwi estimates use the Iwi statistical classification and each iwi grouping (also known as an iwi collective) is defined by the Census iwi grouping . Under the iwi statistical classification, iwi are grouped into geographical regions for statistical purposes.
The information in this page is grouped at the first level of the classification defined by the region.
The number before each iwi name relates to the classification code. The first two digits indicate the region as defined by the classification. For example, Te Aupori has the classification code 0101 indicating this iwi is in the ‘01’ Te Tai Tokerau/Tāmaki-makaurau (Northland/Auckland) region.
Note: this page excludes the classification categories ‘20 Iwi not named, but waka or iwi confederations known’, ‘21 Iwi named, but region unspecified’ and ‘22 Hāpu affiliated with more than one iwi’. This information can be requested through Te Ara Takatū if required.

## View information by region
Te Tai Tokerau/Tāmaki-makaurau (Northland/Auckland) region Hauraki (Coromandel) region Waikato/Te Rohe Pōtae (Waikato/King Country) region Te Arawa/Taupō (Rotorua/Taupō) region Tauranga Moana/Mātaatua (Bay of Plenty) region Te Tai Rāwhiti (East Coast) region Te Matau-a-Māui/Wairarapa (Hawke's Bay/Wairarapa) region Taranaki region Whanganui/Rangitīkei (Wanganui/Rangitīkei) region Manawatū/Horowhenua/Te Whanganui-a-Tara (Manawatū/Horowhenua/Wellington) region Te Waipounamu/Wharekauri (South Island/Chatham Islands) region Iwi groupings

## Te Tai Tokerau/Tāmaki-makaurau (Northland/Auckland) region
The following graph shows the percent of each data source that makes up the 2018 iwi estimates. While data is higher quality when it is sourced from the 2018 Census responses, we do not recommend using only the 2018 Census responses as the resulting counts are too low and more likely to under-represent groups missed by the 2018 Census.

### Key issues for data users for these iwi
- Ngāti Kahu (0102) from the 2013 Census has been separated into Ngāti Kahu (0102) and Te Paatu (0117) in the 2018 Census.
- Ngāpuhi (0104) from the 2013 Census has been separated into Ngāpuhi (0104) and Ngāti Hine (Te Tai Tokerau) (0116) in the 2018 Census.
- Population estimates for Ngāpuhi ki Whaingaroa-Ngāti Kahu ki Whaingaroa (0105) are substantially higher than expected based on 2013 Census, increasing from 2050 in 2013 Census to 4740 in 2018 Census. No coding or questionnaire changes have been identified that may be contributing to the large increase. Comparisons between 2018 Census and 2013 Census proportions should be looked at with extra caution, as changes may be due to the change in who is now counted in Ngāpuhi ki Whaingaroa-Ngāti Kahu ki Whaingaroa (0105).
- The classification for Ngāti Whātua has changed as part of the 2017 iwi classification change. Ngāti Whātua o Ōrākei (0114) and Ngāti Whātua o Kaipara (0113) were included as part of Ngāti Whātua (0109) in 2013 Census but they are now listed separately. Ngāti Whātua (0109) was updated to Ngāti Whātua (not Ōrākei or Kaipara) (0109). The classification change has caused a decrease in counts in Ngāti Whātua (not Ōrākei or Kaipara) (0109) in 2018 Census compared with Ngāti Whātua (0109) in 2013 Census, as some individuals are now counted in Ngāti Whātua o Ōrākei (0114) and Ngāti Whātua o Kaipara (0113). The classification change meant that it was not possible to use data from 2013 Census for people missing iwi affiliation data in 2018 Census. A different estimation approach was used for the three iwi, which increased the contribution made to iwi count via the weighting. Comparisons between 2018 Census and 2013 Census proportions should be looked at with extra caution for Ngāti Whātua (not Ōrākei or Kaipara) (0109), as changes may be due to the change in who is now counted in Ngāti Whātua (not Ōrākei or Kaipara) (0109).
- Te Kawerau ā Maki (0110) was classified as Te Kawerau (0110) in the 2013 Census.
- Population estimates for Te Uri-o-Hau (0111) are lower than expected based on 2013 Census, with 2018 Census estimates showing only a small increase compared with 2013 Census counts. No coding or questionnaire changes have been identified during census evaluation.
- Ngāi Tai ki Tāmaki (0115) was classified as Ngāi Tai (Hauraki) (0209) in the 2013 Census.
- Ngāti Manuhiri (0118) is a new classification in the 2018 Census. It was classified as part of Ngāti Kahungunu, region unspecified (0705) in the 2013 Census. Ngāti Kahungunu, region unspecified (0705) from the 2013 Census was separated into Ngāti Manuhiri (0118) and Ngāti Kahungunu, region not known (2112) in the 2018 Census.
- Ngati Rehua (0119) is a new classification in 2018 Census. It was classified as part of Waikato/Te Rohe Pōtae (Waikato/King Country) region, not further defined (0300) in the 2013 Census. Waikato/Te Rohe Pōtae (Waikato/King Country) region, not further defined (0300) from the 2013 Census has been separated into Ngati Rehua (0119) and Waikato/Te Rohe Pōtae region, iwi not named (2303) in the 2018 Census.

### Key issues for data users for these iwi
- Ngāti Maniapoto (0302) from the 2013 Census has been separated into Ngāti Maniapoto (0302) and Rereahu (0307) in the 2018 Census.
- The classification name for Raukawa (Waikato) (0303) was changed from Ngāti Raukawa (Waikato) (0303) as part of the 2017 iwi classification review. Raukawa (Waikato) (0303) is around 4,000 people lower than expectations based on 2013 Census counts. There have been no significant changes to the coding for Raukawa (Waikato) since 2013 Census, so this does not account for the lower count. If respondents typed 'Ngāti Raukawa' when completing the 2018 Census form they were presented with the suggestion of 'Ngāti Raukawa (Horowhenua/Manawatū)' but not 'Raukawa (Waikato)'. This may have caused some people who affiliate with Raukawa (Waikato) (0303) to be classified under Ngāti Raukawa (Horowhenua/Manawatū) (1004) rather than Raukawa (Waikato) (0303). Comparisons between 2018 Census and 2013 Census proportions should be looked at with extra caution, as changes may be due to the change in who is now counted in Raukawa (Waikato) (0303).
- 2018 Census included approximately 17,000 iwi affiliation responses where Tainui, iwi not named (2001) was specified as the iwi and Waikato as the region. These responses have been coded to Waikato (0304) to be consistent with the coding approach used in the 2013 Census.
- Waikato (0304) from the 2013 Census has been separated into Waikato (0304), Ngāti Te Ata (0305), Ngāti Hīkairo (0306), Ngāti Tiipa (0308), Ngāti Korokī Kahukura (0309), Ngāti Tamaoho (0310), and Te Ākitai-Waiohua (0311) in the 2018 Census.

### Key issues for data users for these iwi
- The size of Te Arawa iwi grouping is in line with expectations based on 2013 Census. However, the residual category Te Arawa, iwi not named (2002), has shown a substantial increase compared to 2013 Census. Subsequently, more specifically coded iwi within Te Arawa iwi grouping have not increased as much as expected, showing only a small increase compared to the 2013 count. Subsequently counts for Ngāti Rangiteaorere (Te Arawa) (0402), Ngāti Rangitihi (Te Arawa) (0403), Ngāti Rangiwewehi (Te Arawa) (0404), Tūhourangi (Te Arawa) (0407), Uenuku-Kōpako (Te Arawa) (0408), and Ngāti Whakaue (Te Arawa) (0410) have not increased as much as expected compared with the 2013 Census counts.
- Ngāti Tūwharetoa (0411) from the 2013 Census has been separated out into Ngāti Tūwharetoa (ki Taupō) (0411), Ngāti Tūwharetoa ki Kawerau (0513) and Ngāti Tūwharetoa, region not known (2113) in the 2018 Census.
- Ngāti Mākino (0413) is a new classification in the 2018 Census. It was classified as part of Te Arawa/Taupō (Rotorua/Taupō) Region, not further defined (0400) in the 2013 Census.
- Ngāti Kearoa / Ngāti Tuarā (0414) is a new classification in the 2018 Census. It was classified as part of Te Arawa/Taupō (Rotorua/Taupō) Region, not further defined (0400) in the 2013 Census.
- Ngāti Rongomai (Te Arawa) (0415) is a new classification in the 2018 Census. It was classified as part of Te Arawa/Taupō (Rotorua/Taupō) Region, not further defined in the 2013 Census.

### Key issues for data users for these iwi
- Ngāi Te Rangi (0502) was classified as Ngaiterangi (0502) in the 2013 Census.
- Ngāi Tai (Tauranga Moana/Mātaatua) (0506) from the 2013 Census has been separated into Ngāi Tai (Tauranga Moana/Mātaatua) (0506) and Ngāi Tai, region not known (2111) in the 2018 Census.
- Whakatōhea (0508) from the 2013 Census has been separated into Whakatōhea (0508) and Te Upokorehe (0512) in the 2018 Census.
- Te Whānau-ā-Apanui (0509) was classified as Te Whānau-a-Apanui (0509) in the 2013 Census.
- Tauranga Moana/Mātaatua (Bay of Plenty) region, not further defined (0500) from the 2013 Census has been separated into Ngā Pōtiki ā Tamapahore (0511) and Tauranga Moana/Mātaatua region, iwi not named (2305) in the 2018 Census.
- Ngāti Tūwharetoa ki Kawerau (0513) is a new classification in the 2018 Census. It was classified as part of Ngāti Tūwharetoa (0411) in the 2013 Census. Ngāti Tūwharetoa (0411) from the 2013 Census was separated into Ngāti Tūwharetoa (ki Taupō) (0411), Ngāti Tūwharetoa ki Kawerau (0513), and Ngāti Tūwharetoa, region not known (2113) in the 2018 Census.

### Key issues for data users for these iwi
- The size of the Ngāti Kāhugnunu grouping is in line with expectations based on 2013 Census. The residual category, Ngāti Kahungunu, region not known (2112), has shown a large decrease compared with 2013. Subsequently, some more specifically classified iwi within the Ngāti Kahungunu grouping have shown large increases. There have been large increases in the counts of Ngāti Kahungunu ki Tamatea (0708), Ngāti Kahungunu ki Tamakinui a Rua (0709), and Ngāti Kahungunu ki Heretaunga (0703). These increases may be due to the introduction of as-you-type suggestions in the online questionnaire supporting a greater number of respondents to select specific Ngāti Kahungunu responses in the 2018 Census. Ngāti Kahungunu, region not known (2112) remains a large residual category with over 18,000 responses and is likely to be important in analysis.
- For Ngāti Kahungunu ki Heretaunga (0703), Ngāti Kahungunu ki Tamatea (0708), and Ngāti Kahungunu ki Tamakinui a Rua (0709), comparisons between 2018 Census and 2013 Census proportions should be looked at with extra caution, as changes may be due to the change in who is now counted in these iwi.
- There has been a change in the coding for Ngāti Kahungunu ki Te Wairoa (0702) since the 2013 Census. In the 2018 Census, if a person responded with 'Ngāti Kahungunu – Hawkes Bay' they were classified as Ngāti Kahungunu – region not known (2112). In the 2013 Census, the response 'Ngāti Kahungunu – Hawkes Bay' was coded to Ngāti Kahungunu ki Te Wairoa (0702). This change affected the coding of around 1,900 records.
- Rangitāne (Te Matau-a-Māui/Hawke's Bay/Wairarapa) (0706) from the 2013 Census has been separated into Rangitāne (Te Matau-a-Māui/Hawke's Bay/Wairarapa) (0706) and Rangitāne o Tamaki nui ā Rua (0714) in the 2018 Census.
- Ngāti Kahungunu ki Te Whanganui-a-Orotu (0707) from the 2013 Census has been separated into Ngāti Kahungunu ki Te Whanganui-a-Orotu (0707) and Ngāti Hineuru (0712) in the 2018 Census.
- Maungaharuru Tangitū (0713) is a new classification in the 2018 Census. It was classified as part of Te Aitanga-a-Māhaki (0602) in the 2013 Census. 2013 Census data could not be used to fill missing data gaps for Maungaharuru Tangitū (0713). A different estimation approach was used that increased the contribution made to the iwi count via the non-response weighting.
- Ngāti Ruapani ki Waikaremoana (0715) was added to the iwi classification in 2017, so it does not have a separate classification in the 2013 Census data. It was classified as part of Hapū affiliated to more than one iwi (2200) in the 2013 Census.
- Te Hika o Pāpāuma (0716) is a new classification in the 2018 Census. It was classified as part of Ngāti Kahungunu ki Tamakinui a Rua (0709) in the 2013 Census. 2013 Census data could not be used to fill missing data gaps for Te Hika o Pāpāuma (0716). A different estimation approach was used that increased the contribution made to the iwi count via the non-response weighting.
The following graph shows the percent of each data source that makes up the 2018 iwi estimates. While data is higher quality when it is sourced from the 2018 Census responses, we do not recommend using only the 2018 Census responses as the resulting counts are too low and more likely to under-represent groups missed by the 2018 Census.

### Key issues for data users for these iwi
- Population estimates for Ngāti Maru (Taranaki) (0802) are substantially higher than expected based on 2013 Census. No coding or questionnaire changes have been identified that may be contributing to the large increase. Comparisons between 2018 Census and 2013 Census proportions should be looked at with extra caution, as changes may be due to the change in who is now counted in Ngāti Maru (Taranaki) (0802).

### Key issues for data users for these iwi
- Ngāti Hauiti (Rangitīkei) (0904) was classified as Ngāti Hauiti (0904) in the 2013 Census.
- Ngāti Whitikaupeka (Rangitīkei) (0905), Ngāi Te Ohuake (Rangitīkei) (0906), and Ngāti Tamakōpiri (Rangitīkei) (0907) are new classifications in the 2018 Census. They were classified as part of Manawatū/Horowhenua/Te Whanganui-a-Tara(Manawatū/Horowhenua/Wellington) region nfd (1000) in the 2013 Census. 2013 Census data could not be used to fill missing data gaps for these iwi. A different estimation approach was used which increased the contribution made to the iwi count via the non-response weighting.
- Ngāti Rangi (Ruapehu, Whanganui) (0908), Uenuku (Ruapehu, Waimarino) (0909), and Tamakana (Ruapehu, Waimarino) (0911) were added to the iwi classification in 2017, so they do not have separate classifications in the 2013 Census data. They were classified as part of Hapū affiliated to more than one iwi (2200) in the 2013 Census.
- Tamahaki (Ruapehu, Waimarino) (0910) is a new classification in the 2018 Census. It was classified as part of Hapū affiliated to more than one iwi (2200) in the 2013 Census. 2013 Census data could not be used to fill missing data gaps for Tamahaki (Ruapehu, Waimarino) (0910). A different estimation approach was used which increased the contribution made to the iwi count via the non-response weighting.

### Key issues for data users for these iwi
- The count for Ngāti Raukawa (Horowhenua/Manawatū) (1004) may include some individuals from Raukawa (Waikato) (0303). The classification name for Raukawa (Waikato) (0303) was changed from Ngāti Raukawa (Waikato) (0303) as part of the 2017 iwi classification review. Raukawa (Waikato) (0303) is around 4,000 people lower than expected based on 2013 Census counts. There have been no significant changes to the coding for Raukawa (Waikato) since 2013 Census, so this does not account for the lower count. If respondents typed 'Ngāti Raukawa' when completing the 2018 Census form they were presented with the suggestion of 'Ngāti Raukawa (Horowhenua/Manawatū)' but not 'Raukawa (Waikato)'. This may have caused some people who affiliate with Raukawa (Waikato) (0303) to be classified under Ngāti Raukawa (Horowhenua/Manawatū) (1004) rather than Raukawa (Waikato) (0303). Comparisons between 2018 Census and 2013 Census proportions should be looked at with extra caution, as changes may be due to the change in who is now counted in Ngāti Raukawa (Horowhenua/Manawatū) (1004).
- The synonyms used to code responses to Ngāti Toarangatira (Te Whanganui-a-Tara/Wellington) (1005) had substantial updates since the 2013 Census. Five synonyms were moved from Ngāti Toarangatira (Te Whanganui-a-Tara/Wellington) (1005) to Ngāti Toa – region not known (2108), and eight synonyms were added to Ngāti Toarangatira (Te Whanganui-a-Tara/Wellington) (1005). These changes are expected to improve the accuracy of coding. Comparisons between 2018 Census and 2013 Census proportions should be looked at with extra caution, as changes may be due to the change in who is now counted in Ngāti Toarangatira (Te Whanganui-a-Tara/Wellington) (1005).
- Population estimates for Te Atiawa ki Whakarongotai (1006) are lower than expected based on 2013 Census, with 2018 Census showing only a small increase compared with 2013 Census counts. No coding or questionnaire changes have been identified during census evaluation.
- Ngāti Tukorehe (1009) is a new classification in the 2018 Census. It was classified as part of Manawatū/Horowhenua/Te Whanganui-a-Tara (Manawatū/Horowhenua/Wellington) region nfd (1000) in the 2013 Census. Manawatū/Horowhenua/Te Whanganui-a-Tara (Manawatū/Horowhenua/Wellington) region nfd (1000) from the 2013 Census was separated into Ngāti Whitikaupeka (Rangitīkei) (0905), Ngāi Te Ohuake (Rangitīkei) (0906), Ngāti Tamakōpiri (Rangitīkei) (0907), Ngāti Tukorehe (1009), and Manawatū/Horowhenua/Te Whanganui-a-Tara region, iwi not named (2310) in the 2018 Census.

### Key issues for data users for these iwi
- Population estimates for Ngāti Toarangatira (Te Waipounamu/South Island) (1111) are substantially higher than expected based on the 2013 Census, moving from 321 in 2013 Census to 909 in 2018 Census. This is due to changes made since the 2013 Census to more accurately assign respondents to Ngāti Toarangatira (Te Waipounamu/South Island (1111) based on their census response. Comparisons between 2018 Census and 2013 Census proportions should be looked at with extra caution, as changes may be due to the change in who is now counted in Ngāti Toarangatira (Te Waipounamu/South Island) (1111).
- Population estimates for Waitaha (Te Waipounamu/South Island) (1112) are substantially higher than expected based on 2013 Census, moving from 1,041 in 2013 Census to 1,704 in 2018 Census. No coding or questionnaire changes have been identified that may be contributing to the large increase. Comparisons between 2018 Census and 2013 Census proportions should be looked at with extra caution, as changes may be due to the change in who is now counted in Waitaha (Te Waipounamu/South Island) (1112).
